---
title: "Journal Entry 4: Chapters 1 and 2 Reflections"
date: "2026-03-01"
categories: [reflection, writing, introduction, related-work]
description: "Reflections on writing the Introduction and Related Work chapters"
---

![Writing reflection](../thumbnail.jpg)

## Reflections on Writing Chapters 1 and 2
In drafting Chapters 1 (Introduction) and 2 (Related Work) I focused on
making the evaluation problem concrete and reproducible. The Introduction
frames a dual-track, file-driven experiment (clean vs. mutated forks)
that keeps the project grounded in a runnable artifact rather than a
purely conceptual benchmark. That decision made it easier to describe
exact experimental steps and to connect methods to measurable metrics.

The literature review organized prior work into four practical lenses:
LLM code generation, adversarial/robustness testing, evaluation
frameworks/benchmarks, and program representation. This helped me
select sources that informed design choices (e.g., mutation-testing
methodology, automated testing tools, and robustness evaluation
frameworks) and to clearly show where the agent-fork design fills a gap
in existing evaluations.

Challenges and feedback: the trickiest task was balancing background
and specificity—readers needed enough context to appreciate mutation
testing, but not so much detail that the introduction lost its
contribution. Early reviewers asked for clearer statements of
assumptions (toy workloads, test-driven validation) and for explicit
threats to validity; I incorporated that feedback by tightening the
scope and calling out limitations up front.

Overall, these two chapters set the experimental agenda and provide the
literature scaffolding needed to interpret the results in a broader
research context.
